file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env zsh

# ========================================
# Main zsh configuration
# ========================================

# home-manager session variables
HM_SESSION_VARS="$HOME/.local/state/home-manager/gcroots/current-home/home-path/etc/profile.d/hm-session-vars.sh"
if [ -f "$HM_SESSION_VARS" ]; then
  . "$HM_SESSION_VARS"
fi

# Enable completion
autoload -Uz compinit && compinit

# git-wt
eval "$(git wt --init zsh)"

# ========================================
# Load config files
# ========================================

# oh-my-zsh configuration (must be loaded first)
source ~/.config/zsh/config/oh-my-zsh.zsh

# zsh plugins
source ~/.zsh/plugins/zsh-abbr/zsh-abbr.plugin.zsh
source ~/.zsh/plugins/zsh-autosuggestions/zsh-autosuggestions.zsh
source ~/.zsh/plugins/zsh-syntax-highlighting/zsh-syntax-highlighting.zsh
source ~/.zsh/plugins/fzf-tab/fzf-tab.plugin.zsh

# ========================================
# Load functions
# ========================================

for func in ~/.config/zsh/functions/*.zsh; do
  source "$func"
done

# aliases & abbreviations (must be after functions loop and zsh-abbr)
source ~/.config/zsh/config/aliases.zsh

# zoxide (must be after oh-my-zsh to avoid cd being overwritten)
eval "$(zoxide init zsh --cmd cd)"

# direnv (must be last to avoid spurious unloading messages)
eval "$(direnv hook zsh)"

content.thinking:
The issue is that zsh-abbr and zsh-syntax-highlighting interfere with each other. This is a known issue. The typical problem is:

1. When you type an abbreviation like `cl`, syntax highlighting colors it red (unknown command) because it hasn't been expanded yet
2. After expansion, the highlighting updates but the visual feedback during typing is confusing

The common solution is to make sure zsh-syntax-highlighting is loaded AFTER zsh-abbr, which is already the case here (line 27 vs 29).

But the real issue might be that abbreviations show up as red (error) before they're expanded. There's a known way to fix this by adding the abbreviations to the syntax highlighting's command list.


I should check if there's already a syntax highlighting configuration in place that I can modify.

content.text:
よくある問題ですね。abbr で入力した略語が、展開前に zsh-syntax-highlighting によって「不明なコマンド」として赤くハイライトされる現象だと思います。

これは zsh-syntax-highlighting にカスタムハイライトルールを追加することで解決できます。zsh-abbr には `ABBR_SET_EXPANSION_CURSOR` など設定がありますが、ハイライト問題に対しては **`ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP`** や **コマンドリストへの追加** で対応するのが一般的です。

最もシンプルな解決策は、`zshrc` で abbreviation を「有効なコマンド」としてハイライトさせる設定を追加することです。

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env zsh

# ========================================
# Abbreviations (zsh-abbr) & Aliases
# ========================================

# rebuild - unified via flake apps
abbr -S -qq rebuild="nix run ~/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/dotnix#switch && source ~/.zshrc"

# Platform-specific abbreviations
if [[ "$(uname)" == "Darwin" ]]; then
  abbr -S -qq cpd="pwd | pbcopy"
else
  abbr -S -qq cpd="pwd | wl-copy"
fi

# AI tools
abbr -S -qq cl="claude"
abbr -S -qq cld="claude --dangerously-skip-permissions"
abbr -S -qq cldc="claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --continue"
abbr -S -qq clh="claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --model haiku"
abbr -S -qq clo="claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --model opus"
abbr -S -qq cls="claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --model sonnet"
abbr -S -qq cg="cage"
abbr -S -qq cgcl="cage claude"
abbr -S -qq cgcx="cage codex"
abbr -S -qq oc="opencode"
abbr -S -qq cx="codex"
abbr -S -qq ca="cursor-agent"
abbr -S -qq ct="continues"

# Docker
abbr -S -qq do="docker container"
abbr -S -qq dop="docker container ps"
abbr -S -qq dob="docker container build"
abbr -S -qq dor="docker container run --rm"
abbr -S -qq dox="docker container exec -it"

# Docker Compose
abbr -S -qq dc="docker compose"
abbr -S -qq dcu="docker compose up"
abbr -S -qq dcub="docker compose up --build"
abbr -S -qq dcd="docker compose down"
abbr -S -qq dcr="docker compose restart"

# Nix
abbr -S -qq ns="nix-shell"
abbr -S -qq ngc="nix-collect-garbage"
nrn() { nix run "nixpkgs#$1" "${@:2}"; }
abbr -S -qq ashiba="nfi github:yutakobayashidev/ashiba"
abbr -S -qq ndt="nfi github:the-nix-way/dev-templates"

# Security
scorecard() { GITHUB_AUTH_TOKEN="$(gh auth token)" command scorecard "$@"; }

abbr -S -qq p="pnpm"
abbr -S -qq gg="ghq get"

# Command replacements (keep as aliases — no visual expansion needed)
alias ls="lsd -1A"
alias ll="lsd -l"
alias la="lsd -la"
alias lt="lsd --tree"
alias cat="bat"

# Git
abbr -S -qq browse-pr="gh pr view --web"
alias isv='gh issue list | fzf-tmux --prompt "issue preview>" --preview "echo {} | awk '\''{print \$1}'\'' | xargs gh issue view -p" | xargs gh issue view'
alias prv='gh pr list | fzf-tmux --prompt "pr preview>" --preview "echo {} | awk '\''{print \$1}'\'' | xargs gh pr view -p" | xargs gh pr view'
abbr -S -qq browse="gh repo view --web"
alias brc='echo "$(gh repo view --json url --jq .url)/commit/$(git rev-parse HEAD)"'

# ========================================
# Main zsh configuration
# ========================================

# home-manager session variables

  . "$HM_SESSION_VARS"

# git-wt

# ========================================

# ========================================

Here are the relevant findings on the **zsh-abbr** and **zsh-syntax-highlighting** conflict/integration:

## The Problem

When using `alias gst="git status"`, zsh-syntax-highlighting shows if it is a valid command/alias. But when defining the same with zsh-abbr, it looks invalid. The question is whether it's possible to get the same validation with zsh-abbr.

## The Fix: Using the Regexp Highlighter

The recommended solution comes from [GitHub Issue #20](https://github.com/olets/zsh-abbr/issues/20):

Enable the regexp highlighter and add a pattern that matches abbreviations as 'words' to highlight them as you like:

```zsh
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_HIGHLIGHTERS=(main regexp)
# highlight known abbreviations
typeset -A ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP
# regexp matches abbreviations only when they are a standalone word, but not part of other words
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP+=('(^| )('${(j:|:)${(k)ABBR_REGULAR_USER_ABBREVIATIONS}}')($| )' 'fg=blue')
```


## Bonus: Fixing Autosuggestions After Abbreviations

Also, autosuggesting abbreviations doesn't make much sense as they are usually just 2-3 symbols. You can disable autosuggestions after abbreviations with:

```zsh
ZSH_AUTOSUGGEST_HISTORY_IGNORE=${(j:|:)${(k)ABBR_REGULAR_USER_ABBREVIATIONS}}
ZSH_AUTOSUGGEST_COMPLETION_IGNORE=${ZSH_AUTOSUGGEST_HISTORY_IGNORE}
```


## Plugin Load Order Matters

When using oh-my-zsh, zsh-abbr needs to be sourced after any `bindkey -v` line in `.zshrc`. The solution is simply to move the `source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh` line to the bottom of the file.

## Modern zsh-abbr Has Built-in Support

The latest version of zsh-abbr describes itself as having a "full-featured CLI; dotfiles-friendly; integrates with suggestions and syntax highlighting; comprehensive documentation; configurable for power users." So newer versions may handle this integration out of the box.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Full-featured CLI; dotfiles-friendly; integrates with suggestions and syntax highlighting; comprehensive documentation; configurable for power users; surfaces core features for extension authors. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/olets/zsh-abbr"},{"title":"ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP regex expression not working · Issue #56 · olets/zsh-abbr","url":"https://github.com/olets/zsh-abbr/issues/56"},{"title":"snippets for zsh-autosuggestions and zsh-syntax-highlighting · Issue #20 · olets/zsh-abbr","url":"https://github.com/olets/zsh-abbr/issues/20"},{"title":"zsh-abbr","url":"https://zsh-abbr.olets.dev/"},{"title":"Possible to integrate with zsh-syntax-highlighting? · Issue #23 · olets/zsh-abbr","url":"https://github.com/olets/zsh-abbr/issues/23"},{"title":"fast-syntax-highlighting highlighting of multi-word abbreviations · Issue #120 · olets/zsh-abbr","url":"https://github.com/olets/zsh-abbr/issues/120"},{"title":"fast-syntax-highlighting highlighting of single-word abbreviations · Issue #24 · olets/zsh-abbr","url":"https://github.com/olets/zsh-abbr/issues/24"},{"title":"zsh-abbr-v4-docs/docs/advanced.md at main · olets/zsh-abbr-v4-docs","url":"https://github.com/olets/zsh-abbr-v4-docs/blob/main/docs/advanced.md"},{"title":"Zsh Abbr | Fig","url":"https://fig.io/plugins/other/zsh-abbr_olets"}]

Here are the relevant results for your query about **zsh-abbr syntax highlighting abbreviations being colored wrong**:

## Key Findings

### The Core Problem

When using an alias, e.g. `alias gst="git status"`, zsh-syntax-highlighting shows if it is a valid command/alias. But when defining the same with zsh-abbr, it looks invalid. The question is whether it's possible to get the same validation with zsh-abbr.

This is because zsh-syntax-highlighting doesn't natively recognize zsh-abbr abbreviations as valid commands, so they appear with the "unknown command" color (typically red).

### Solution: Using the `regexp` Highlighter

To highlight user abbreviations that will expand, zsh-syntax-highlighting users can add these lines to `.zshrc` below where zsh-abbr is loaded. For more info see the zsh-syntax-highlighting regexp highlighter documentation. Replace `<styles for global abbreviations>` with a zsh character highlighting string. For example `fg=blue`, `fg=blue,bg=red,bold`, etc.

The recommended configuration from the zsh-abbr docs (v4):

```zsh
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_HIGHLIGHTERS+=(regexp)
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP+=('^[[:blank:][:space:]]*('${(j:|:)${(k)ABBR_REGULAR_USER_ABBREVIATIONS}}')$' <styles for regular abbreviations>)
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP+=('\<('${(j:|:)${(k)ABBR_GLOBAL_USER_ABBREVIATIONS}}')$' <styles for global abbreviations>)
```

### Known Issue: Regex Not Working on Linux

Some users reported those regex expressions aren't working on Linux. The `echo` of the abbreviation keys gives the expected array, but they are not getting matched and highlighted. The user was calling the `ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP` setup after sourcing zsh-abbr and declaring the highlighters according to the zsh-syntax-highlighting documentation.

### Alternative Snippet from the Community

Another approach: enable the regexp highlighter and add a pattern that matches abbreviations as 'words' to highlight them as you like, using a regex that "matches abbreviations only when they standalone word, but not part of other words."

### fast-syntax-highlighting Support

fast-syntax-highlighting highlighting of single-word abbreviations was done in issue #24. However, fast-syntax-highlighting highlighting of **multi-word** abbreviations is still an open enhancement request (issue #120).

### The Core Problem

When using an alias, e.g. `alias gst="git status"`, zsh-syntax-highlighting shows if it is a valid command/alias. But when defining the same with zsh-abbr, it looks invalid. The question is whether it's possible to get the same validation with zsh-abbr.

This is because zsh-syntax-highlighting doesn't natively recognize zsh-abbr abbreviations as valid commands, so they appear with the "unknown command" color (typically red).

### Solution: Using the `regexp` Highlighter

To highlight user abbreviations that will expand, zsh-syntax-highlighting users can add these lines to `.zshrc` below where zsh-abbr is loaded. For more info see the zsh-syntax-highlighting regexp highlighter documentation. Replace `<styles for global abbreviations>` with a zsh character highlighting string. For example `fg=blue`, `fg=blue,bg=red,bold`, etc.

The recommended configuration from the zsh-abbr docs (v4):

```zsh
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_HIGHLIGHTERS+=(regexp)
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP+=('^[[:blank:][:space:]]*('${(j:|:)${(k)ABBR_REGULAR_USER_ABBREVIATIONS}}')$' <styles for regular abbreviations>)
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP+=('\<('${(j:|:)${(k)ABBR_GLOBAL_USER_ABBREVIATIONS}}')$' <styles for global abbreviations>)
```

### Known Issue: Regex Not Working on Linux

Some users reported those regex expressions aren't working on Linux. The `echo` of the abbreviation keys gives the expected array, but they are not getting matched and highlighted. The user was calling the `ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP` setup after sourcing zsh-abbr and declaring the highlighters according to the zsh-syntax-highlighting documentation.

### Alternative Snippet from the Community

Another approach: enable the regexp highlighter and add a pattern that matches abbreviations as 'words' to highlight them as you like, using a regex that "matches abbreviations only when they standalone word, but not part of other words."

### fast-syntax-highlighting Support

fast-syntax-highlighting highlighting of single-word abbreviations was done in issue #24. However, fast-syntax-highlighting highlighting of **multi-word** abbreviations is still an open enhancement request (issue #120).

#!/usr/bin/env zsh

# ========================================
# Main zsh configuration
# ========================================

# home-manager session variables
HM_SESSION_VARS="$HOME/.local/state/home-manager/gcroots/current-home/home-path/etc/profile.d/hm-session-vars.sh"
if [ -f "$HM_SESSION_VARS" ]; then
  . "$HM_SESSION_VARS"
fi

# Enable completion
autoload -Uz compinit && compinit

# git-wt
eval "$(git wt --init zsh)"

# ========================================
# Load config files
# ========================================

# oh-my-zsh configuration (must be loaded first)
source ~/.config/zsh/config/oh-my-zsh.zsh

# zsh plugins
source ~/.zsh/plugins/zsh-abbr/zsh-abbr.plugin.zsh
source ~/.zsh/plugins/zsh-autosuggestions/zsh-autosuggestions.zsh
source ~/.zsh/plugins/zsh-syntax-highlighting/zsh-syntax-highlighting.zsh
source ~/.zsh/plugins/fzf-tab/fzf-tab.plugin.zsh

# ========================================
# Load functions
# ========================================

for func in ~/.config/zsh/functions/*.zsh; do
  source "$func"
done

# aliases & abbreviations (must be after functions loop and zsh-abbr)
source ~/.config/zsh/config/aliases.zsh

# zoxide (must be after oh-my-zsh to avoid cd being overwritten)
eval "$(zoxide init zsh --cmd cd)"

# direnv (must be last to avoid spurious unloading messages)
eval "$(direnv hook zsh)"

lines: # aliases & abbreviations (must be after functions loop and zsh-abbr)
+# Highlight abbreviations as valid commands for zsh-syntax-highlighting

content.thinking: The user is asking what `abbr -S` means. Let me explain.

content.text:
`abbr -S` は **セッション略語** (Session abbreviation) を作成するフラグです。

| フラグ | 種類 | 保存先 | 永続性 |
|--------|------|--------|--------|
| なし / `-U` | **ユーザー略語** | `~/.config/zsh-abbr/user-abbreviations` ファイル | シェル再起動後も残る |
| `-S` | **セッション略語** | メモリ上のみ | シェル終了で消える |

あなたの `aliases.zsh` では毎回 `abbr -S -qq` でセッション略語として登録しているので、シェル起動のたびに再登録されています。

ちなみに `-qq` は出力を完全に抑制するフラグです。

正直なところ、毎回 `-S` でセッション登録する理由は特にないので、`abbr` (ユーザー略語) に切り替えれば：

1. **起動が速くなる** — 毎回の再登録が不要
2. **syntax highlighting 連携が楽** — 公式のドキュメントが `ABBR_REGULAR_USER_ABBREVIATIONS` 前提で書かれている

ただしその場合、略語の管理が `aliases.zsh` ファイルではなく zsh-abbr の内部ファイルに移ることになるので、dotfiles で宣言的に管理したい場合は今のまま `-S` のほうが都合が良いです。

message.content: なんのための機能？

content.text:
一時的にそのシェルセッションだけで使いたい略語のための機能です。

例えば、あるプロジェクトで作業中に一時的に `abbr -S tf="terraform plan"` と定義して、そのターミナルを閉じたら自動的に消える、という使い方です。

あなたの `aliases.zsh` のように dotfiles で宣言的に管理するケースでは `-S` を使う意味はほぼありません。毎回起動時に再登録するオーバーヘッドがあるだけです。ユーザー略語 (`abbr` without `-S`) に切り替えたほうが素直な使い方になります。

Actually, for user abbreviations, we should use `abbr` command once to register them, and they persist in `~/.config/zsh-abbr/user-abbreviations`. So we shouldn't put them in aliases.zsh at all - or we need to use a different approach.


I'm looking at the available options here. I could run the `abbr` commands once to register everything and then clean up aliases.zsh, or I could check if zsh-abbr has built-in handling for this scenario. The idiomatic approach with zsh-abbr for dotfiles is probably to use something like `abbr import-aliases` or define them in a way that handles duplicates gracefully.

The cleanest solution seems to be using the `--force` flag to overwrite existing abbreviations without errors, which would make them persistent user abbreviations and avoid issues when re-sourcing the config. I need to figure out the right approach for managing these abbreviations declaratively. The issue is that if I define them in the dotfiles, they need to persist to the user abbreviations file, but I also want changes to be reflected when the config reloads. Using `abbr --force` would work—it overwrites existing abbreviations each time, which means the dotfiles stay as the source of truth even though it's a bit redundant on subsequent runs.

I should also update the syntax highlighting configuration to use the user abbreviations variable instead of session abbreviations, then apply these changes to both files.

#!/usr/bin/env zsh

# ========================================
# Main zsh configuration
# ========================================

# home-manager session variables
HM_SESSION_VARS="$HOME/.local/state/home-manager/gcroots/current-home/home-path/etc/profile.d/hm-session-vars.sh"
if [ -f "$HM_SESSION_VARS" ]; then
  . "$HM_SESSION_VARS"
fi

# Enable completion
autoload -Uz compinit && compinit

# git-wt
eval "$(git wt --init zsh)"

# ========================================
# Load config files
# ========================================

# oh-my-zsh configuration (must be loaded first)
source ~/.config/zsh/config/oh-my-zsh.zsh

# zsh plugins
source ~/.zsh/plugins/zsh-abbr/zsh-abbr.plugin.zsh
source ~/.zsh/plugins/zsh-autosuggestions/zsh-autosuggestions.zsh
source ~/.zsh/plugins/zsh-syntax-highlighting/zsh-syntax-highlighting.zsh
source ~/.zsh/plugins/fzf-tab/fzf-tab.plugin.zsh

# ========================================
# Load functions
# ========================================

for func in ~/.config/zsh/functions/*.zsh; do
  source "$func"
done

# aliases & abbreviations (must be after functions loop and zsh-abbr)
source ~/.config/zsh/config/aliases.zsh

# Highlight abbreviations as valid commands for zsh-syntax-highlighting
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_HIGHLIGHTERS+=(regexp)
typeset -gA ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP+=('^[[:blank:][:space:]]*('${(j:|:)${(k)ABBR_REGULAR_SESSION_ABBREVIATIONS}}')$' 'fg=blue')
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP+=('\<('${(j:|:)${(k)ABBR_GLOBAL_SESSION_ABBREVIATIONS}}')$' 'fg=blue')

# zoxide (must be after oh-my-zsh to avoid cd being overwritten)
eval "$(zoxide init zsh --cmd cd)"

# direnv (must be last to avoid spurious unloading messages)
eval "$(direnv hook zsh)"

#!/usr/bin/env zsh

# ========================================
# Abbreviations (zsh-abbr) & Aliases
# ========================================

# rebuild - unified via flake apps
abbr -S -qq rebuild="nix run ~/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/dotnix#switch && source ~/.zshrc"

# Platform-specific abbreviations
if [[ "$(uname)" == "Darwin" ]]; then
  abbr -S -qq cpd="pwd | pbcopy"
else
  abbr -S -qq cpd="pwd | wl-copy"
fi

# AI tools
abbr -S -qq cl="claude"
abbr -S -qq cld="claude --dangerously-skip-permissions"
abbr -S -qq cldc="claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --continue"
abbr -S -qq clh="claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --model haiku"
abbr -S -qq clo="claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --model opus"
abbr -S -qq cls="claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --model sonnet"
abbr -S -qq cg="cage"
abbr -S -qq cgcl="cage claude"
abbr -S -qq cgcx="cage codex"
abbr -S -qq oc="opencode"
abbr -S -qq cx="codex"
abbr -S -qq ca="cursor-agent"
abbr -S -qq ct="continues"

# Docker
abbr -S -qq do="docker container"
abbr -S -qq dop="docker container ps"
abbr -S -qq dob="docker container build"
abbr -S -qq dor="docker container run --rm"
abbr -S -qq dox="docker container exec -it"

# Docker Compose
abbr -S -qq dc="docker compose"
abbr -S -qq dcu="docker compose up"
abbr -S -qq dcub="docker compose up --build"
abbr -S -qq dcd="docker compose down"
abbr -S -qq dcr="docker compose restart"

# Nix
abbr -S -qq ns="nix-shell"
abbr -S -qq ngc="nix-collect-garbage"
nrn() { nix run "nixpkgs#$1" "${@:2}"; }
abbr -S -qq ashiba="nfi github:yutakobayashidev/ashiba"
abbr -S -qq ndt="nfi github:the-nix-way/dev-templates"

# Security
scorecard() { GITHUB_AUTH_TOKEN="$(gh auth token)" command scorecard "$@"; }

abbr -S -qq p="pnpm"
abbr -S -qq gg="ghq get"

# Command replacements (keep as aliases — no visual expansion needed)
alias ls="lsd -1A"
alias ll="lsd -l"
alias la="lsd -la"
alias lt="lsd --tree"
alias cat="bat"

# Git
abbr -S -qq browse-pr="gh pr view --web"
alias isv='gh issue list | fzf-tmux --prompt "issue preview>" --preview "echo {} | awk '\''{print \$1}'\'' | xargs gh issue view -p" | xargs gh issue view'
alias prv='gh pr list | fzf-tmux --prompt "pr preview>" --preview "echo {} | awk '\''{print \$1}'\'' | xargs gh pr view -p" | xargs gh pr view'
abbr -S -qq browse="gh repo view --web"
alias brc='echo "$(gh repo view --json url --jq .url)/commit/$(git rev-parse HEAD)"'
abbr -S -qq gundo="git reset --soft HEAD~1"
abbr -S -qq grepush="git recommit && git push -f"
abbr -S -qq gsp="git stash && git pull && git stash pop"
abbr -S -qq gpgp="git pull && git push"
abbr -S -qq gpu="git pull"
abbr -S -qq rmswap='find . -type f -name "*.swp" -delete && find . -type f -name ".*.swp" -delete'

I see the problem! The pattern has quotes around the abbreviation keys: `"gundo"|"gsp"|...` instead of `gundo|gsp|...`. The `${(k)ABBR_REGULAR_USER_ABBREVIATIONS}` expansion includes the quotes. So the regex pattern won't match because when you type `cl`, it's matching against `"cl"` in the regex.

Also the global abbreviations pattern is `\<()$` which is empty (no global abbreviations defined), and `\<` might not work on Linux anyway.

Let me fix this by stripping the quotes. I need to use parameter expansion to remove quotes.


I'm modifying the zshrc to strip those quotes from the keys so the pattern matches correctly.

#!/usr/bin/env zsh

# ========================================
# Main zsh configuration
# ========================================

# home-manager session variables
HM_SESSION_VARS="$HOME/.local/state/home-manager/gcroots/current-home/home-path/etc/profile.d/hm-session-vars.sh"
if [ -f "$HM_SESSION_VARS" ]; then
  . "$HM_SESSION_VARS"
fi

# Enable completion
autoload -Uz compinit && compinit

# git-wt
eval "$(git wt --init zsh)"

# ========================================
# Load config files
# ========================================

# oh-my-zsh configuration (must be loaded first)
source ~/.config/zsh/config/oh-my-zsh.zsh

# zsh plugins
source ~/.zsh/plugins/zsh-abbr/zsh-abbr.plugin.zsh
source ~/.zsh/plugins/zsh-autosuggestions/zsh-autosuggestions.zsh
source ~/.zsh/plugins/zsh-syntax-highlighting/zsh-syntax-highlighting.zsh
source ~/.zsh/plugins/fzf-tab/fzf-tab.plugin.zsh

# ========================================
# Load functions
# ========================================

for func in ~/.config/zsh/functions/*.zsh; do
  source "$func"
done

# aliases & abbreviations (must be after functions loop and zsh-abbr)
source ~/.config/zsh/config/aliases.zsh

# Highlight abbreviations as valid commands for zsh-syntax-highlighting
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_HIGHLIGHTERS+=(regexp)
typeset -gA ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP+=('^[[:blank:][:space:]]*('${(j:|:)${(k)ABBR_REGULAR_USER_ABBREVIATIONS}}')$' 'fg=blue')
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP+=('\<('${(j:|:)${(k)ABBR_GLOBAL_USER_ABBREVIATIONS}}')$' 'fg=blue')

# zoxide (must be after oh-my-zsh to avoid cd being overwritten)
eval "$(zoxide init zsh --cmd cd)"

# direnv (must be last to avoid spurious unloading messages)
eval "$(direnv hook zsh)"

Here are the results for integrating **zsh-abbr** with syntax highlighting, based on the official GitHub repository:

## Official Solution for Syntax Highlighting Integration

zsh-abbr brings auto-expanding abbreviations to your zsh terminal, and it integrates with suggestions and syntax highlighting.

### Issue Background

When using an alias like `alias gst="git status"`, zsh-syntax-highlighting shows if it is a valid command/alias. When defining the same with zsh-abbr, it looks invalid. This prompted the question of whether it's possible to get the same validation with zsh-abbr.

### Official Solution (zsh-syntax-highlighting)

To highlight user abbreviations that will expand, zsh-syntax-highlighting users can add lines to `.zshrc` below where zsh-abbr is loaded. For more info, see the zsh-syntax-highlighting regexp highlighter documentation. Replace `<styles for ...>` with a zsh character highlighting string, for example `fg=blue`, `fg=blue,bg=red,bold`, etc.

The recommended snippet (from the v4 docs) is:

```zsh
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_HIGHLIGHTERS+=(regexp)
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP+=('^[[:blank:][:space:]]*('${(j:|:)${(k)ABBR_REGULAR_USER_ABBREVIATIONS}}')$' <styles for regular abbreviations>)
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP+=('\<('${(j:|:)${(k)ABBR_GLOBAL_USER_ABBREVIATIONS}}')$' <styles for global abbreviations>)
```

### Community Snippet (from Issue #20)

A community member noticed the TODO items about autosuggestions and highlighting and shared snippets to better work with zsh-abbr. The approach enables the regexp highlighter and adds a pattern that matches abbreviations as standalone words:

```zsh
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_HIGHLIGHTERS=(main regexp)
typeset -A ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP
ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP+=('(^| )('${(j:|:)${(k)ABBR_REGULAR_USER_ABBREVIATIONS}}')($| )' 'fg=blue')
```

### fast-syntax-highlighting Support

fast-syntax-highlighting highlighting of single-word abbreviations was done in issue #24. There is also a tracking issue (#120) for extending support to multi-word abbreviations.

It would be nice to support fast-syntax-highlighting, whose chroma docs are available for reference.

### Vi Mode Note

Switching to vi mode — with plain old `bindkey -v` or with a vi/Vim mode plugin — will wipe out the keybindings zsh-abbr's interactive behavior relies on. If you use vi mode, enable it **before** initializing zsh-abbr.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

## Official Solution for Syntax Highlighting Integration

### Issue Background

For more info, see the zsh-syntax-highlighting regexp highlighter documentation. content.text:
公式のパッチはなく、今やっているような **ユーザー側での設定** が公式の推奨方法です。

[公式ドキュメント（v4）](https://github.com/olets/zsh-abbr-v4-docs/blob/main/docs/advanced.md) が提示しているスニペットがまさに今の設定と同じものですが、キーにクォートが含まれる問題は公式では触れられていません。

ただし [Issue #24](https://github.com/olets/zsh-abbr/issues/24) で **fast-syntax-highlighting** との統合は zsh-abbr 側で対応済みとのことです。`fast-syntax-highlighting` に切り替えれば、手動のワークアラウンドなしでそのまま動きます。

切り替えますか？

Sources:
- [zsh-abbr README](https://github.com/olets/zsh-abbr)
- [Issue #23 - Possible to integrate with zsh-syntax-highlighting?](https://github.com/olets/zsh-abbr/issues/23)

- [Issue #24 - fast-syntax-highlighting highlighting of single-word abbreviations](https://github.com/olets/zsh-abbr/issues/24)

message.content: キーにクォートが含まれる問題とは

content.text:
zsh-abbr の `ABBR_REGULAR_USER_ABBREVIATIONS` の連想配列に格納されるキーが、`cl` ではなく `"cl"` のようにダブルクォートで囲まれた状態になっている問題です。

さっきの確認で見えた通り：

```
# 公式ドキュメントが想定している形
pattern: (cl|dc|gpu)$

# 実際に展開された形
pattern: ("cl"|"dc"|"gpu")$
```

なので公式のスニペットをそのまま貼っても正規表現が `cl` にマッチせず、ハイライトが効きません。先ほどの修正で `keys=(${keys//\"/})` としてクォートを除去したのはそのためです。

zsh-abbr 側のバグか仕様変更かは不明ですが、公式ドキュメントではこの点に言及がありません。

git add zsh/config/aliases.zsh zsh/zshrc && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
zsh: switch abbr from session to user abbreviations and fix syntax highlighting

- Change `abbr -S` (session) to `abbr -f` (user, force) so abbreviations
  persist without re-registering every shell startup
- Add regexp highlighter to color abbreviations as valid commands (fg=blue)
  instead of unknown command (red), working around quoted keys in
  ABBR_REGULAR_USER_ABBREVIATIONS

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text:
D1 の設定で `"remote": true` になっているので、ローカルではなくリモートの D1 に接続しています。リモート DB にはテーブルが存在するはずなので、接続自体に問題がありそうです。

ローカル開発で動かすなら、マイグレーションをローカル D1 に適用する必要があります。

```bash
cd /home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app
npx wrangler d1 migrations apply radar --local
```

を実行するか、あるいは意図的にリモート DB を使いたい場合はリモート側にマイグレーションが適用済みか確認してください。

どちらで進めますか？

content.thinking:
The user wants to add NIP-07 login support to their Nostr feed app. NIP-07 is a Nostr protocol that allows web applications to interact with browser extensions (like nos2x, Alby) to get the user's public key and sign events.

Let me understand the current setup and plan the changes:

1. Currently, the app has hardcoded follows in `nostr-config.ts`
2. They want NIP-07 login (browser extension) to get the user's pubkey
3. Then build the timeline from that user's follow list (kind:3 contact list)

Let me look at the current code structure more carefully and plan the implementation.


Now I need to examine the existing code to understand how to integrate NIP-07 login, fetch the user's contact list from Nostr relays, and dynamically populate the timeline based on their follows instead of using hardcoded values. I should also update the UI to reflect the login state.

content.thinking:
OK, I need to:

1. Replace the hardcoded follows with NIP-07 login
2. After login, fetch the user's contact list (kind:3) to get their follows
3. Build the timeline from those follows

Changes needed:
- `nostr-config.ts`: Remove hardcoded follows, keep relays
- `useNostr.ts`: Accept pubkeys as parameter instead of using hardcoded follows
- New hook or logic for NIP-07 login + fetching contact list
- `nostr.tsx`: Add login button, manage auth state

Let me implement this.

NIP-07 works by checking `window.nostr` which provides:


- `getPublicKey()` - returns hex pubkey
- `signEvent(event)` - signs an event

The flow is: user clicks "Login with Extension", I call `window.nostr.getPublicKey()` to get their hex pubkey, fetch kind:3 (contact list) for that pubkey to get their follows, then build the timeline from those follows' kind:1 notes.

content.thinking:
The issue is likely that `createRxOneshotReq` completes too quickly or the contact list request hangs and never completes. The `await new Promise` might never resolve if there's no kind:3 event for the user.

The problem is that `createRxOneshotReq` may not emit a `complete` signal if the relay doesn't have the data, causing the Promise to hang forever.

Let me fix this by adding a timeout, and also consider using `createRxForwardReq` with a manual timeout instead. Or better yet, use a different approach - use `createRxBackwardReq` for the one-shot query.
 
Actually, I think the real issue is that I'm using `createRxForwardReq` for historical data when I should be using `createRxBackwardReq` instead. `createRxForwardReq` only listens for new events arriving after the subscription starts, so it won't fetch the user's existing contact list from the relays. That's why the follows aren't being found even though the pubkey is retrieved successfully.

content.text: 問題がわかりました。`createRxForwardReq` はリアルタイムの新規イベントのみを待つので、既存のノートが取得できません。過去のノート取得には `createRxBackwardReq` を使う必要があります。また、コンタクトリストの `createRxOneshotReq` もタイムアウトがないと永遠に待ち続ける可能性があります。

message.content: https://trap.jp/post/2414/ この記事参考に色々改善して

input.prompt:
この記事の技術的な内容をすべて詳細に抽出してください。特に以下の点を重点的に：
1. rx-nostrの使い方（createRxNostr, createRxBackwardReq, createRxForwardReq, createRxOneshotReqなど）
2. タイムライン構築のベストプラクティス
3. イベントのフィルタリング、購読の仕方
4. プロフィール取得の効率的な方法
5. リレー接続の管理
6. パフォーマンス最適化のテクニック
7. コード例があればそのまま含めてください

content.content:
# 技術的な内容の抽出

## 1. rx-nostrの使用方法

記事では以下のAPIが紹介されています：

- **createRxNostr**: REQサブスクリプション管理用のオブジェクト作成
- **createRxBackwardReq**: REQメッセージ生成オブジェクト
- **batch**: 複数REQをまとめる（1秒ごとにバッチ化）
- **uniq**: 重複イベント除去

```typescript
const rxNostr = createRxNostr({...});
const rxBackwardReq = createRxBackwardReq();
rxNostr
  .use(rxBackwardReq.pipe(
    bufferWhen(() => interval(1000)),
    batch((a, b) => mergeSimilarAndRemoveEmptyFilters([...a, ...b]))
  ))
  .pipe(uniq())
  .subscribe({next: (e) => cacheAndEmitRelatedEvent(e, emit, setter)})
```

## 2. イベント取得の仕組み

記事に示される実装パターン：

- **フィルターマージ**: REQ数削減のため複数フィルターを統合
- **キャッシング**: イベント受信時に「set」メソッドで保存
- **関連イベント再帰取得**: 投稿者情報、リアクション、リプライを同時取得

## 3. パフォーマンス最適化

主要な工夫：
- バッチ処理による「複数REQのフィルターをマージ」
- キャッシュ層による重複取得防止
- EOSE完了判定を利用した段階的読込み

## 4. 課題と制限

記事では「複数リレー×複数REQ×非同期レスポンス」の複雑性を指摘。特にloading状態取得の困難さについて言及されています。

詳細実装は公開リポジトリ参照が推奨されています。

# 技術的な内容の抽出

## 1. rx-nostrの使用方法

記事では以下のAPIが紹介されています：

- **createRxNostr**: REQサブスクリプション管理用のオブジェクト作成
- **createRxBackwardReq**: REQメッセージ生成オブジェクト
- **batch**: 複数REQをまとめる（1秒ごとにバッチ化）
- **uniq**: 重複イベント除去

```typescript
const rxNostr = createRxNostr({...});
const rxBackwardReq = createRxBackwardReq();
rxNostr
  .use(rxBackwardReq.pipe(
    bufferWhen(() => interval(1000)),
    batch((a, b) => mergeSimilarAndRemoveEmptyFilters([...a, ...b]))
  ))
  .pipe(uniq())
  .subscribe({next: (e) => cacheAndEmitRelatedEvent(e, emit, setter)})
```

## 2. イベント取得の仕組み

記事に示される実装パターン：

- **フィルターマージ**: REQ数削減のため複数フィルターを統合
- **キャッシング**: イベント受信時に「set」メソッドで保存
- **関連イベント再帰取得**: 投稿者情報、リアクション、リプライを同時取得

## 3. パフォーマンス最適化

主要な工夫：
- バッチ処理による「複数REQのフィルターをマージ」
- キャッシュ層による重複取得防止
- EOSE完了判定を利用した段階的読込み

## 4. 課題と制限

記事では「複数リレー×複数REQ×非同期レスポンス」の複雑性を指摘。特にloading状態取得の困難さについて言及されています。

詳細実装は公開リポジトリ参照が推奨されています。

# NostrのクライアントをSolidJSで自作してみた

## 概要

d_etteiu8383氏による、分散型SNS Nostrのウェブクライアント「Streets」開発についての詳細記事です。SolidJS、Ark UI、UnoCSS、Valibotなどの技術スタックと、Nostr固有のイベント送受信処理の実装方法について解説されています。

## Nostrプロトコルの基礎

### イベント構造

Nostrのイベントは以下のJSON形式で表現されます：

```json
{
  "id": "シリアライズしたイベントのsha256",
  "kind": "0-35535の整数でイベント種別",
  "tags": "string[][]形式の付加情報",
  "content": "イベントの内容",
  "created_at": "unixタイムスタンプ",
  "pubkey": "ユーザーID",
  "sig": "署名"
}
```

kindの例：
- kind 0: ユーザープロフィール
- kind 1: ショートテキスト（ツイート相当）
- その他多数の種別が定義されている

### REQ/イベント受信メカニズム

クライアント側からリレーへ送信するREQメッセージ：

```javascript
["REQ", <subscription_id>, <filter1>, <filter2>, ...]
```

フィルター例：

```javascript
{
  "authors": ["pubkey_list"],
  "kinds": [1],
  "limit": 10,
  "since": unix_timestamp,
  "until": unix_timestamp,
  "#<letter>": ["tag_values"]
}
```

リレーからの応答：

```javascript
["EVENT", <subscription_id>, <event_object>]
["EOSE", <subscription_id>]  // End Of Stored Events
```

### 非同期処理の複雑性

重要な特性として、「EOSEを受信した後も、その後投稿されたイベントは同じsubscription IDで送信される」という点があります。これにより「N個のREQ × M個の非同期レスポンス × L個のリレー」という複雑な処理が発生します。

## 技術スタック

### SolidJS

記事では「SolidJSに不満ないよ俺」とコメントされており、特にtype narrowingの強化を評価しています。stateに関する制約の少なさが利点として挙げられています。

### UnoCSS

"utility-first"なスタイリングで、headless UIライブラリとの相性が良好。tree-shakingでバンドルサイズを削減できます。

### Kobalte と Ark UI

- **Kobalte**: headless UIライブラリ。シンプルな設計が好まれています
- **Ark UI**: より多くのコンポーネント提供。画像アップロード部分で使用

Ark UIのcontextパターン例：

```typescript
import { useFileUpload } from '@ark-ui/solid/file-upload'

const fileUpload = useFileUpload({ maxFiles: 5 })

const handleClear = () => {
  fileUpload.clearFiles()
}
```

### Valibot

バリデーションライブラリ。メソッドチェーンではなく独立した関数を使用するため、tree-shakingが効き「バンドルサイズを減らせることが特徴」です。Modular Formsとの相性が良好。

## イベント取得実装の進化

### 初期アプローチ：TanStack Query

Promise ベースの`createQuery`を使用。queryFnで REQ 送信とイベント受信を一箇所で行い、EOSE 後のイベント追加は`queryClient.setQueryData()`で実装。

この実装の限界：
- Nostrの非同期イベント受信形態とPromiseの相性が悪い
- スケーリングに伴い管理が困難化

### 改良版：独自イベントストア + rx-nostr

Promiseを使用しない独自ストア設計：

```typescript
type CacheKey = (string | number | boolean | undefined)[]

const get = (props: () => { 
  queryKey: CacheKey, 
  emitter: () => void 
}): () => unknown => {
  // ストアからイベント(のaccessor)を取得
}

const set = (
  queryKey: CacheKey,
  data: unknown | ((prev: unknown) => unknown),
): void => {
  // ストアにイベントを追加
}
```

SolidJSの`createStore`と`createEffect`で実装。invalidateは以下のタイミングで実行：
- ストアにデータがない時
- データが古い時
- invalidateが明示的に呼ばれた時

### rx-nostr統合パターン

RxJSを使用したイベント送受信の実装：

```typescript
import {
  batch,
  createRxBackwardReq,
  createRxNostr,
  uniq,
} from "rx-nostr"

const rxNostr = createRxNostr({ /* config */ })
const rxBackwardReq = createRxBackwardReq()
const emit = rxBackwardReq.emit
const setter = eventCacheSetter()

rxNostr
  .use(
    rxBackwardReq.pipe(
      bufferWhen(() => interval(1000)),  // 1秒ごとにREQをまとめる
      batch((a, b) => mergeSimilarAndRemoveEmptyFilters([...a, ...b])), // フィルターマージ
    ),
  )
  .pipe(uniq())  // 重複除去
  .subscribe({
    next: (e) => {
      cacheAndEmitRelatedEvent(e, emit, setter)
    },
  })
```

#### 各オペレータの役割

- **bufferWhen**: 1秒間にREQメッセージをバッファリング
- **batch**: フィルターをマージしてREQ数を削減
- **uniq**: 重複を除去
- **cacheAndEmitRelatedEvent**: イベントをキャッシュに保存しつつ、関連イベント（投稿者情報、リアクション、リプライ等）を再帰的に取得

#### 関連イベント取得の流れ

テキストイベント表示には以下の関連情報が必要：
- 投稿者のプロフィール（kind 0）
- リアクション一覧（kind 7）
- リポスト（kind 6）
- リプライ（kind 1 with e タグ参照）

`cacheAndEmitRelatedEvent`内でこれらを一括してemitし、効率的に取得します。

#### EOSE（Fetching状態）の問題と解決

フィルターをマージするため、特定フィルターの loading 状態を取得できないという課題がありました。

解決策として、EOSE が必要なリクエストについてはマージをあきらめ、個別に`createRxBackwardReq()`を作成：

```typescript
const emitWithEOSE = (...props: Parameters<typeof emit>) => {
  return new Promise<void>((resolve) => {
    const backwardReq = createRxBackwardReq()
    rxNostr.use(backwardReq.pipe(batchReq)).subscribe({
      complete() {
        resolve()
      },
    })
    backwardReq.emit(...props)
    backwardReq.over()
  })
}
```

## 実装上の課題と対策

### 画像サイズの最適化

Nostrは投稿にURLのみを含めるため、メタデータやサムネイルがありません。画像圧縮ライブラリ`browser-image-compression`を使用してクライアント側で対応。

### CORS対応

OGP取得時のCORS制限を`corsproxy.io`で回避。将来的には自前プロキシ + 画像最適化プロキシの構築を計画中。

### イベント信頼性

NIP で "mandatory" の`content`フィールドもvalidationされていないため、基本的にすべてのプロパティを nullable として扱う必要があります。

### クリック可能性

投稿要素にクリックハンドラを付ける際、ネストされたリンクやボタンとの衝突を避けるため、`onClick`内で clicked 要素の種類を愚直に確認：

```typescript
// 内容は検証のため省略（ベストプラクティス不明）
// リンク要素かボタン要素の区別、ネスト状態の確認を実装
```

## 開発ツール・ワークフロー

### i18n Ally

VSCode 拡張。inline annotation で翻訳文を表示。カスタムフレームワーク設定例：

```yaml
# .vscode/i18n-ally-custom-framework.yml
languageIds:
  - javascript
  - typescript
  - javascriptreact
  - typescriptreact

usageMatchRegex:
  - "\\Wt\\(\\s*['\"`]({key})['\"`]"

monopoly: true
```

### ローカルリレー環境

開発環境では Docker で以下を構築：
- **nostr-rs-relay**: リレーサーバー
- **Nostrcheck server**: ファイルアップロード機能

### リリースフロー

GitHub Actions でタグ作成時に `release` ブランチへの PR を自動生成。変更内容を機械的にリストアップして影響範囲を明確化。

## その他の所感

- **traQ**: 部内製 SNS のクライアント実装が参考になった
- **SolidJS 開発体験**: state の制約が少なく快適
- **バリデーション**: Valibot + Modular Forms の組み合わせで再レンダリング最適化が自動

## まとめ

Nostr の特殊なイベント送受信形態に対して、初期の Promise ベース実装から RxJS を活用した Reactive 実装へシフトすることで、スケーラブルなクライアント設計を実現しました。フロントエンド開発に関する総合的な学習機会となり、SolidJS の開発体験の優秀さを改めて認識させられた案件となっています。

content.thinking:
Great, now I have a good understanding of the article. The key improvements I should implement:

1. **Batch REQ requests** - Use `bufferWhen` + `batch` to merge similar filters and reduce REQ count
2. **Dedup with `uniq()`** - Use rx-nostr's `uniq()` pipe to deduplicate events
3. **Related event fetching** - When receiving a note (kind:1), automatically fetch the author's profile (kind:0), reactions (kind:7), reposts (kind:6)
4. **Efficient profile fetching** - Don't fetch profiles we already have cached


5. **EOSE handling** - Use `createRxBackwardReq` with `.over()` properly for loading states

Looking at the current implementation, I can see several inefficiencies: there's no batching happening so each profile fetch sends its own REQ, deduplication isn't being used, and the global feed loads profiles one-by-one as notes arrive instead of batching them together. I'm also missing reaction and repost data entirely.

I'll start by adding `uniq()` to all subscriptions and then implement batching for profile requests in the global feed by collecting pubkeys before making requests.

3. **リアクション数の取得** — kind:7 を購読してノートにリアクション数を表示
4. **リポスト数の取得** — kind:6 を購読

content.text:
push しました。`2157d37`

記事を参考に実装した改善点：

1. **`uniq()` パイプ** — 全購読に追加。複数リレーからの重複イベントを排除
2. **`bufferWhen` + `batch`** — プロフィール取得REQを1秒ごとにバッファリング・マージしてリレーへの負荷を軽減
3. **`ensureProfile()` パターン** — 未取得のpubkeyのみプロフィールを遅延取得、キャッシュ済みはスキップ
4. **リアクション・リポスト取得** — kind:7（リアクション）とkind:6（リポスト）を購読し、ノートカードに表示
5. **プロフィールの重複更新防止** — `handleProfile` で既にキャッシュにあれば Map を再生成しない

content.thinking:
The user wants to integrate Nostr users into their Radar category system. They want to tag Nostr users with categories and show their posts in the corresponding Radar category columns. They want to embed this information on the Nostr side.

Let me think about how Nostr works and what options are available:

1. **NIP-51 Lists (kind:30000)** - Custom lists that can tag pubkeys with labels
2. **NIP-32 Labels (kind:1985)** - Labeling events/pubkeys with categories
3. **Custom tags on kind:3 (contact list)** - Could add custom tags to follow list entries


4. **Custom event kind** - Create a custom kind to store category mappings

NIP-51 Lists seem like the most natural fit here—I can create named lists corresponding to each Radar category, with each list containing pubkeys of users in that category. NIP-32 Labels would be more flexible but also more complex to work with.

So the structure would be kind:30000 events where the "d" tag holds the category slug and "p" tags reference the pubkeys, all stored on Nostr relays under the user's control. I should walk through this approach with the user before diving into implementation.

content.text:
いいアイデアですね。Nostr のプロトコル上でこれを実現するなら、いくつかのアプローチがあります。

## 選択肢

### 1. NIP-51 Lists (kind:30000) — おすすめ

Radar のカテゴリごとに「リスト」を作って、pubkey を紐付ける。

```json
{
  "kind": 30000,
  "tags": [
    ["d", "tech"],
    ["p", "<pubkey1>"],
    ["p", "<pubkey2>"]
  ]
}
```

- `d` タグ = Radar のカテゴリスラッグ
- `p` タグ = そのカテゴリに属する Nostr ユーザー
- **Parameterized Replaceable Event** なので更新可能
- オーナーの秘密鍵で署名 → リレーに保存 → どこからでも取得可能

### 2. NIP-32 Labels (kind:1985)

pubkey にラベルを貼る形式。より汎用的だが、リスト管理には少し冗長。

### 3. カスタム kind

独自の kind を定義する。自由度は高いが、エコシステムとの互換性がない。

---

**NIP-51 Lists** が一番 Radar のカテゴリ構造と相性がいいと思います。フローとしては：

1. NIP-07 でログイン（オーナーとして）
2. カテゴリごとの kind:30000 リストをリレーから取得
3. Radar のカテゴリページでそのリストの pubkey の投稿を表示
4. 管理画面でリストの編集（pubkey の追加/削除）もできる

この方向で進めますか？

The user wants to add notification and reaction sidebars/columns to the Nostr deck. Let me think about what they need:

1. **Notifications** - mentions (kind:1 with "p" tag pointing to owner), replies (kind:1 with "e" tag referencing owner's notes)
2. **Reactions** - kind:7 events targeting the owner's notes

These should be additional deck columns. Let me implement both.

For notifications:
- kind:1 with `#p` filter for owner's pubkey (mentions/replies to them)

For reactions:
- kind:7 with `#p` filter for owner's pubkey (reactions to their notes)


- Also including kind:6 (reposts) as part of notifications

I notice the current code already subscribes to reactions and reposts for stats, but I need to store these as individual events for the new columns. I'll add state for notifications and reactions, set up the subscriptions for the relevant event kinds, and create the new deck columns to display them.

I'll tackle the notifications and reactions columns first since they're more straightforward deck additions, then move on to NIP-51 list support. For notifications, I need to capture mentions, reactions to my notes, and reposts. The reactions column will focus on displaying reactions received on my content.

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The conversation covers two main projects:
   
   **A. dotnix (zsh config)**: Fix interference between zsh-abbr and zsh-syntax-highlighting. Migrate from session abbreviations (`abbr -S`) to user abbreviations (`abbr -f`). Add regexp highlighter to color abbreviations correctly.
   
   **B. feed-app (Nostr client)**: Build a Nostr feed page with TweetDeck-style deck layout integrated into the existing Radar app. Features include: NIP-07 login, timeline from owner's npub contact list, posting capability for owner, notifications, reactions, global feed. The latest request is to implement **NIP-51 Lists (kind:30000)** to tag Nostr users with Radar categories and integrate Nostr content into the Radar category pages.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - **zsh-abbr**: `abbr -S` (session) vs `abbr -f` (user, force overwrite), `ABBR_REGULAR_USER_ABBREVIATIONS` variable has quoted keys
   - **zsh-syntax-highlighting**: `regexp` highlighter, `ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_REGEXP` patterns
   - **Nostr protocol**: Events (kind:0 profile, kind:1 note, kind:3 contacts, kind:6 repost, kind:7 reaction, kind:30000 NIP-51 lists)
   - **rx-nostr**: `createRxNostr`, `createRxBackwardReq` (historical), `createRxForwardReq` (real-time), `uniq()` (dedup across relays), `batch()` + `bufferWhen(interval(1000))` (REQ merging), `.over()` (signal EOSE completion), `rxNostr.send()` (publish events)
   - **NIP-07**: Browser extension API (`window.nostr.getPublicKey()`, `window.nostr.signEvent()`)
   - **NIP-51 Lists**: kind:30000 parameterized replaceable events with `d` tag as category slug and `p` tags as pubkeys
   - **React patterns**: `useMemo` for derived deck data, `useCallback` for stable functions, `useRef` for rxNostr instance
   - **Cloudflare D1 + Drizzle ORM**: Existing Radar data layer

3. Files and Code Sections:

   - **`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app/app/hooks/useNostr.ts`**
     - Core hook managing all Nostr state and subscriptions
     - Exports: `NostrNote`, `NostrProfile`, `NoteStats`, `NostrNotification`, `NostrReaction` types
     - Key patterns: batched profile fetching, `ensureProfile()`, `addNote()` helper, `publish()` for NIP-07 signing
     - Current state includes: notes, globalNotes, notifications, reactions, noteStats, profiles, follows, signerPubkey, ownerHex, isOwner
     - Uses `rxNostrRef` to hold rxNostr instance for publishing

   - **`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app/app/components/feed/NostrTimeline.tsx`**
     - Components: `NostrNoteCard`, `NostrTimeline`, `NostrDeckColumn`, `NostrNotificationColumn`, `NostrReactionColumn`
     - `NostrDeckColumn` accepts `headerContent` prop for custom headers (profile card, compose form)
     - `NotificationCard` shows icon by type (mention/reaction/repost), actor, timestamp
     - `ReactionCard` shows emoji, actor name

   - **`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app/app/routes/nostr.tsx`**
     - 6-column deck: My Notes, Timeline, Tagged, Notifications, Reactions, Global
     - `ComposeForm` component (textarea + Post button) shown only when `isOwner`
     - My Notes header: owner profile card + NIP-07 login/logout button
     - Uses `AppLayout` with `isDeckMode` for correct padding

   - **`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app/app/data/nostr-config.ts`**
     - `NOSTR_RELAYS`: damus, nos.lol, nostr.band
     - `OWNER_NPUB`: `REDACTED`

   - **`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app/app/components/feed/index.ts`**
     - Exports: `CardGrid`, `TweetTimeline`, `LinkifiedText`, `DeckView`, `NostrTimeline`, `NostrDeckColumn`, `NostrNotificationColumn`, `NostrReactionColumn`

   - **`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app/app/components/feed/DeckView.tsx`**
     - Existing Radar deck pattern: w-96 columns, horizontal scroll, `DeckColumn` with infinite scroll
     - Reference pattern for Nostr deck columns

   - **`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app/app/components/layout/AppLayout.tsx`**
     - `isDeckMode` prop controls: `h-screen overflow-hidden` vs `min-h-screen`, and `overflow-hidden flex flex-col` vs `p-4 overflow-y-auto` for main

   - **`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app/app/routes/home.tsx`**
     - Radar home page with DeckView, category filtering, source filtering
     - Uses `categoryList` from `~/data/types` for categories
     - Reference for how category integration should work

   - **`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/dotnix/zsh/zshrc`**
     - Added regexp highlighter config after aliases.zsh, stripping quotes from abbr keys

   - **`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/dotnix/zsh/config/aliases.zsh`**
     - Changed all `abbr -S -qq` to `abbr -f -qq`

4. Errors and fixes:
   - **zsh-syntax-highlighting regexp not working**: Official snippet from zsh-abbr docs didn't work because `ABBR_REGULAR_USER_ABBREVIATIONS` keys contain double quotes (`"cl"` not `cl`). Fixed with `keys=(${keys//\"/})`.
   - **"そもそも色使なくなった" (colors stopped working)**: First attempted fix with just `ZSH_HIGHLIGHT_HIGHLIGHTERS+=(regexp)` before zsh-abbr didn't help. User said to revert. Root cause was the quoted keys issue.
   - **Nostr notes not loading after NIP-07 login**: Used `createRxForwardReq` which only listens for NEW events. Fixed by using `createRxBackwardReq` for historical data + `createRxForwardReq` for real-time.
   - **createRxOneshotReq hanging**: Contact list fetch could hang forever if relay didn't respond. Added 10s timeout and switched to `createRxBackwardReq` with `.over()`.
   - **Wrong npub**: User initially gave `npub129wzvq...` then corrected to `REDACTED`.
   - **Padding mismatch with Radar**: Nostr page didn't pass `isDeckMode` to `AppLayout`, causing extra `p-4` padding. Fixed by adding `isDeckMode` prop.
   - **User said "singinしても何もこラン" (nothing happens after signin)**: Was the `createRxForwardReq` vs `createRxBackwardReq` issue.
   - **User said "なおっとらん" (not fixed)**: After first backward req fix, still not working. Was actually working but user confirmed it was fine.

5. Problem Solving:
   - Diagnosed rx-nostr API differences (backward vs forward vs oneshot req) for proper historical + real-time data fetching
   - Applied optimizations from trap.jp article: `uniq()`, `batch()`, `bufferWhen`, batched profile fetching
   - Solved the deck layout by reusing existing patterns from Radar's `DeckView`
   - Implemented owner-only posting with pubkey comparison (`signerPubkey === ownerHex`)

6. All user messages:
   - "abbrとzshのコマンドハイライトが干渉してしまってわかりづらいんだけど"
   - "やっぱその変更取り消して、治ってない気がする"
   - "そもそも色使なくなった"
   - "Webで調べて"
   - "abbr -Sって何"
   - "なんのための機能？"
   - "直してくれ"
   - "なおっとらん"
   - "公式のパッチないの？"
   - "キーにクォートが含まれる問題とは"
   - "コミットして"
   - (git push output)
   - "Oops! Failed query..." (D1 database error in feed-app)
   - "まぁいいや、コミットしてpush"
   - "singinしても何もこラン"
   - "治ってないね"
   - "ああ、動いてた、直さなくてよかったのかも、何買えたんだっけ"
   - "未ログインでも私のnpubからTL構築するようにしたいな、REDACTED mutate部分だけnip-07のアカウントを使うイメージ"
   - "あ、ごめんこれ、REDACTED"
   - "コミットしてpush"
   - "y"
   - "radarのtop pageみたいにtweetdeckみたいなdeck形にしたい。自分のアカウント、タイムライン、タグみたいなイメージ"
   - "リレー全体のdeck"
   - "外のpaddingがradartop pageと違うよね,Login with NIP-07はMy Notesのところに移して,twitterみたいにアカウントの情報出すようにして"
   - "https://trap.jp/post/2414/ この記事参考に色々改善して"
   - "ログインして私だったらポストもできるようにして"
   - "nostrユーザーにタグ付けしてradarのカテゴリー別ページに統合できたりしないのかな、nostr側に情報埋め込みたいんだけど"
   - "通知とリアクションのdeckも"
   - "push"
   - "NIP-51 Listsやろう"

7. Pending Tasks:
   - **NIP-51 Lists (kind:30000) implementation**: Tag Nostr users with Radar categories using parameterized replaceable events. `d` tag = category slug, `p` tags = pubkeys. Need to:
     1. Fetch owner's kind:30000 lists from relays
     2. Parse category-to-pubkey mappings
     3. Integrate Nostr users' posts into Radar category pages
     4. Add management UI for editing lists (add/remove pubkeys per category, only when logged in as owner)
     5. Publish updated lists via NIP-07 signing

8. Current Work:
   I entered plan mode to design the NIP-51 Lists implementation. The user's request: "nostrユーザーにタグ付けしてradarのカテゴリー別ページに統合できたりしないのかな、nostr側に情報埋め込みたいんだけど" followed by "NIP-51 Listsやろう". The approach agreed upon: use NIP-51 Lists (kind:30000) where each Radar category has a corresponding list with `d` tag as the category slug and `p` tags as Nostr pubkeys. This data lives on Nostr relays, signed by the owner.

9. Optional Next Step:
   Explore the codebase to understand Radar's category system (`categoryList`, `categorySlug`, the home page's deck/category integration) and design the NIP-51 Lists implementation plan. The user said "NIP-51 Listsやろう" and I entered plan mode with `EnterPlanMode`. Need to explore the category system and design the implementation.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /home/yuta/.config/claude/projects/-home-yuta-ghq-github-com-yutakobayashidev-dotnix/d9bd212f-26dc-43c7-a33a-2a5412d0f425.jsonl

Based on the search results, here's information about NIP-51 - the Nostr Lists specification:

## Overview

NIP-51 defines lists of things that users can create. Lists can contain references to anything, and these references can be public or private.

## Structure

Public items in a list are specified in the event tags array, while private items are specified in a JSON array that mimics the structure of the event tags array, but stringified and encrypted using the same scheme from NIP-44 (the shared key is computed using the author's public and private key) and stored in the .content.

## Encryption Standards

An earlier version of this specification used NIP-04 for encryptions. Those are now deprecated. For backward compatibility, Clients can automatically discover if the encryption is NIP-04 or NIP-44 by searching for "iv" in the ciphertext and decrypting accordingly.

## Use Cases

NIP-51 defines how users can create and manage categorized lists for bookmarks, mutes, pins, and other collections on Nostr.

## Event Management

When new items are added to an existing list, clients SHOULD append them to the end of the list, so they are stored in chronological order.

For the complete technical specification, you can refer to the official GitHub repository at https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/blob/master/51.md.

## NIP-51 Overview

NIP-51 defines lists of things that users can create. Lists can contain references to anything, and these references can be public or private.

## List Structure

Public items in a list are specified in the event tags array, while private items are specified in a JSON array that mimics the structure of the event tags array, but stringified and encrypted using the same scheme from NIP-44 (the shared key is computed using the author's public and private key) and stored in the .content.

## Encryption Details

For backward compatibility, clients can automatically discover if the encryption is NIP-04 or NIP-44 by searching for "iv" in the ciphertext and decrypting accordingly. When new items are added to an existing list, clients SHOULD append them to the end of the list, so they are stored in chronological order.

## List Types

Different list types use different event kinds:
- If a list should only be defined once per user (like the "mute" list) the list is declared as a replaceable event, referred to as "replaceable lists".

# NIP-51: Lists Specification Overview

NIP-51 defines a system for creating lists of references with public or private items. Here's what you need to know:

## Core Structure

"This NIP defines lists of things that users can create. Lists can contain references to anything, and these references can be **public** or **private**." Public items appear in event tags, while private ones are encrypted using NIP-44 and stored in the content field.

## Two Main List Categories

**Standard Lists** (kinds 10000-10102): Fixed-purpose lists where users can have only one of each type. Examples include mute lists (kind 10000), bookmarks (10003), and community memberships (10004).

**Sets** (kinds 30000-39092): Multi-instance lists users can create multiple versions of, each with a unique "d" identifier. These include follow sets, relay sets, curation sets, and starter packs.

## Key Technical Details

- Items should be "append[ed] to the end of the list, so they are stored in chronological order"
- Sets can include optional "title," "image," and "description" tags for UI enhancement
- Backward compatibility exists for older NIP-04 encryption; clients detect it by searching for "iv" in ciphertexts

## Common Use Cases

Mute lists suppress unwanted content, bookmark sets organize saved articles, curation sets group topical content, and release artifact sets bundle software distribution files.

Based on the search results, here's information about NIP-51 kind 30000 and 30001 structure with examples:

## Kind 30000 and 30001 Structure

NIP-51 defines lists of things that users can create, where lists can contain references to anything with public or private items specified in the event tags array or encrypted content respectively.

Events with kind 30000 to 40000 are addressable by their kind, pubkey and d tag value, meaning only the latest event for each combination must be stored by relays.

## Example: Kind 30000 (Mute List)

An example kind 30000 event uses a "d" tag with value "nostr" and multiple "p" tags containing pubkey values:

```json
{
  "kind": 30000,
  "tags": [
    ["d", "nostr"],
    ["p", "3bf0c63fcb93463407af97a5e5ee64fa883d107ef9e558472c4eb9aaaefa459d"],
    ["p", "32e1827635450ebb3c5a7d12c1f8e7b2b514439ac10a67eef3d9fd9c5c68e245"]
  ],
  "content": "..."
}
```

## Example: Kind 30001 (Categorized Bookmarks)

An example kind 30001 event for categorized bookmarks includes a "d" tag with value "bookmarks", "e" tags for event references, "a" tags for addressable event references, and "r" tags for URL references.

The "d" tag serves as the identifier for the replaceable list, while "p" and other tags reference the items contained in that list.

## Overview of NIP-51 Lists

An event with kind 10000 is defined as a replaceable list event for listing content a user wants to mute.

## Tag Format

Public items in a list are specified in the event tags array, while private items are specified in a JSON array that mimics the structure of the event tags array, but stringified and encrypted using the same scheme from NIP-44 (the shared key is computed using the author's public and private key) and stored in the .content.

## Encryption Evolution

There was a significant change in the specification regarding encryption: An earlier version of this specification used NIP-04 for encryptions. Those are now deprecated. For backward compatibility, Clients can automatically discover if the encryption is NIP-04 or NIP-44 by searching for "iv" in the ciphertext and decrypting accordingly.

## List Types

Standard lists use normal replaceable events, meaning users may only have a single list of each kind. They have special meaning and clients may rely on them to augment a user's profile or browsing experience.

The search results show that kind 10000 is specifically used for mute lists, with related kinds like 10001 for pin lists and 10003 for bookmark lists.

Based on the search results, here's information about NIP-51 and parameterized replaceable events:

## NIP-51 and Parameterized Replaceable Events

Parameterized replaceable events use the list name as a "d" tag, and these lists may be referred to as "parameterized replaceable lists".

### How They Work

NIP-33 defines parameterized replaceable events (kinds 30000-39999) with unique identifiers, enabling updatable content with stable, addressable references. The "d" tag is required and unique per kind per pubkey, and only the latest event with the same (kind, pubkey, d) is kept—earlier events with the same coordinate are replaced.

### Publishing and Updating

To delete or remove content from a parameterized replaceable event, you publish a new event with the same coordinate and empty/deleted content.

### NIP-51 Lists

Public items in a list are specified in the event tags array, while private items are specified in a JSON array that mimics the structure of the event tags array, but stringified and encrypted using the same scheme from NIP-44. When new items are added to an existing list, clients SHOULD append them to the end of the list, so they are stored in chronological order.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about NIP-51 encrypted content and NIP-44:

## Overview

Public items in a list are specified in the event tags array, while private items are specified in a JSON array that mimics the structure of the event tags array, but stringified and encrypted using the same scheme from NIP-44 (the shared key is computed using the author's public and private key) and stored in the .content.

## Encryption Details

For backward compatibility, Clients can automatically discover if the encryption is NIP-04 or NIP-44 by searching for "iv" in the ciphertext and decrypting accordingly.

## Practical Example

The search results include a code example showing how to implement this:

Private items are structured as a JSON array like `[["p", "07caba282f76441955b695551c3c5c742e5b9202a3784780f8086fdcdc1da3a9"], ["a", "a55c15f5e41d5aebd236eca5e0142789c5385703f1a7485aa4b38d94fd18dcc4"]]`, which is then encrypted using `nip44.encrypt(json.encode_to_string(private_items))` and stored in the event.content field.

The main concept is that NIP-51 lists can contain both public tags (visible in the event's tags array) and private tags (encrypted in the content field using NIP-44's encryption scheme).

Based on the search results, I found information about rx-nostr and related Nostr concepts:

## rx-nostr Overview

rx-nostr is a library that supports high quality and flexible communication with multiple Nostr relays. rx-nostr provides transparent interface to read/write events on Nostr relays. Under the hood, rx-nostr makes use of RxJS, but you don't need to touch it directly.

## Creating an rx-nostr Instance

To use the library, you create an instance using `createRxNostr`. The API accepts a configuration object with options like `signer` (using `seckeySigner`), and `verifier`, along with `websocketCtor` for Node.js environments.

## Event Kind 30000

Regarding event kind 30000 specifically: For kind n such that 30000 <= n < 40000, events are addressable by their kind, pubkey and d tag value -- which means that, for each combination of kind, pubkey and the d tag value, only the latest event MUST be stored by relays, older versions MAY be discarded.

This means kind 30000 events are "replaceable events" that are indexed by their kind, public key, and a "d" tag value, with relays keeping only the most recent version of each unique combination.

The search results don't contain detailed API documentation on the specific method for sending events with kind 30000 using rx-nostr. For more specific API details, you may want to check the official rx-nostr GitHub repository or npm package documentation directly.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have enough information. Let me compile my findings into a comprehensive research report.

## Feed-App Radar Category System Research

I've completed a thorough exploration of the feed-app codebase. Here's what I found:

### 1. **Categories Definition** (`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app/app/data/types.ts`)

The category system is defined as a const list with 12 categories:

```typescript
export const categoryList = [
  { name: "All", slug: "all", color: "bg-gray-100 text-gray-700" },
  { name: "AI/ML", slug: "ai-ml", color: "bg-purple-100 text-purple-700" },
  { name: "Developer Tools", slug: "developer-tools", color: "bg-blue-100 text-blue-700" },
  { name: "Web Standards", slug: "web-standards", color: "bg-green-100 text-green-700" },
  { name: "Security & Privacy", slug: "security-privacy", color: "bg-red-100 text-red-700" },
  { name: "Digital Identity", slug: "digital-identity", color: "bg-indigo-100 text-indigo-700" },
  { name: "Platform & Services", slug: "platform-services", color: "bg-cyan-100 text-cyan-700" },
  { name: "Company Engineering", slug: "company-engineering", color: "bg-orange-100 text-orange-700" },
  { name: "Personal Blog", slug: "personal-blog", color: "bg-pink-100 text-pink-700" },
  { name: "Social Impact", slug: "social-impact", color: "bg-yellow-100 text-yellow-700" },
  { name: "Media & Culture", slug: "media-culture", color: "bg-teal-100 text-teal-700" },
  { name: "Twitter", slug: "twitter", color: "bg-sky-100 text-sky-700" },
] as const;
```

**Type definitions:**
- `CategoryInfo = typeof categoryList[number]`
- `CategoryName = CategoryInfo["name"]`
- `CategorySlug = CategoryInfo["slug"]`

**Utility functions:**
- `getCategoryBySlug(slug: string): CategoryInfo` — returns category info or defaults to "All"
- `getCategoryByName(name: string): CategoryInfo` — returns category info or defaults to "All"

### 2. **Database Schema** (`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app/db/schema.ts`)

Two main tables:

**`sources` table:**
- Stores feed sources with two category fields:
  - `category: text` — full category name (e.g., "AI/ML")
  - `categorySlug: text` — URL-friendly slug (e.g., "ai-ml")
- Each source belongs to exactly one category
- Other fields: id, name, url, description, kind, createdAt, updatedAt

**`radarItems` table:**
- Stores articles, tweets, and content items
- References sources via foreign key
- No direct category field (categories accessed via JOIN with sources table)
- Fields: id, title, source (FK), sourceName, summary, image, url, type, metadata, timestamp, createdAt, updatedAt

### 3. **Category-Related UI Components**

**CategoryBadge** (`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app/app/components/ui/CategoryBadge.tsx`):
- Takes category name as input
- Renders a colored badge using Tailwind classes from categoryList
- Used in CardGrid, TweetCard, and SourcesGrid

**CategoryFilter** (in `home.tsx`):
- Renders category pill buttons in a horizontal scrollable layout
- Only visible in non-deck mode when no source is selected
- Clicking a category updates the URL query param `category=<slug>`

### 4. **Routing and URL Parameters**

**Home page** (`/app/routes/home.tsx`):
- Query parameters:
  - `category` (slug) — filters by categorySlug in sources table
  - `source` (id) — filters by source ID
  - `kind` (articles|releases|video|podcast|twitter) — filters by source.kind
  - `period` (All|Today|Month) — client-side filtering only
- No dedicated category pages — all category filtering is done via query params on the home route

### 5. **Data Flow: D1/Drizzle to Frontend**

**Deck Mode (default landing):**
1. Home loader checks URL params and determines if `isDeckMode` (all filters are "all")
2. If deck mode:
   - Queries all active categories (excluding "all")
   - For each category: runs a parallel query joining `radarItems` + `sources` where `sources.categorySlug = cat.slug`
   - Returns `deckData: DeckData` (record of category slug → {items, hasMore})
3. DeckView renders one column per category with initial items
4. Each DeckColumn can load more items via `/api/radar-items?category=<slug>&page=<n>`

**Filtered Mode (when category/source/kind filter is active):**
1. Home loader builds SQL conditions from query params
2. Queries radarItems + sources with JOINs and WHERE clauses
3. Returns items with category/categorySlug attached from the sources table
4. Client-side pagination via `/api/radar-items` API endpoint
5. Period filtering done client-side after API response

**API Endpoint** (`/app/routes/api/radar-items.ts`):
- Accepts query params: `page`, `category`, `source`, `kind`
- Builds WHERE clause from conditions
- Returns paginated results (20 per page) with category data from JOIN

### 6. **How DeckView Integrates Categories**

DeckView (in `DeckView.tsx`):
- Takes `deckData: DeckData` (a record mapping category slugs to items)
- Filters to only show categories that have items
- Renders one `DeckColumn` per active category
- Each column:
  - Displays category name in header
  - Shows up to 10 initial items
  - Supports lazy-loading more items via Intersection Observer
  - Applies period/kind filters client-side

**DeckData structure:**
```typescript
type DeckData = Record<string, DeckColumnData>;
interface DeckColumnData {
  items: RadarItemWithCategory[];
  hasMore: boolean;
}
```

### Key Insights

1. **Categories are static** — defined in code, not in database
2. **Category assignment** — happens at the source level (each source has a fixed category)
3. **No individual category pages** — categories are accessed only via query params on home route
4. **Dual mode rendering**:
   - **Deck mode**: Kanban-style columns for all active categories (initial load)
   - **Filtered mode**: Grid/timeline for specific category/source/kind
5. **Query params drive filtering**: URL state is preserved and synced via `nuqs` (Next.js URL state library)
6. **Color coding**: Each category has a dedicated Tailwind color for badges throughout the UI
7. **Client-side period filtering**: Period filtering (Today/Month/All) happens after data is fetched, not in the database query

### File Locations Summary

| Purpose | Path |
|---------|------|
| Category definitions & types | `/app/data/types.ts` |
| Database schema | `/db/schema.ts` |
| Home page with deck/filter logic | `/app/routes/home.tsx` |
| API pagination endpoint | `/app/routes/api/radar-items.ts` |
| DeckView component | `/app/components/feed/DeckView.tsx` |
| CategoryBadge UI | `/app/components/ui/CategoryBadge.tsx` |
| Layout with sidebar | `/app/components/layout/AppLayout.tsx`, `/Sidebar.tsx` |
| Card displays | `/app/components/feed/CardGrid.tsx` |

- `getCategoryBySlug(slug: string): CategoryInfo` — returns category info or defaults to "All"
- `getCategoryByName(name: string): CategoryInfo` — returns category info or defaults to "All"

### 2. **Database Schema** (`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app/db/schema.ts`)

Two main tables:

**`sources` table:**
- Stores feed sources with two category fields:
  - `category: text` — full category name (e.g., "AI/ML")
  - `categorySlug: text` — URL-friendly slug (e.g., "ai-ml")
- Each source belongs to exactly one category
- Other fields: id, name, url, description, kind, createdAt, updatedAt

**`radarItems` table:**
- Stores articles, tweets, and content items
- References sources via foreign key
- No direct category field (categories accessed via JOIN with sources table)
- Fields: id, title, source (FK), sourceName, summary, image, url, type, metadata, timestamp, createdAt, updatedAt

### 3. **Category-Related UI Components**

**CategoryBadge** (`/home/yuta/ghq/github.com/yutakobayashidev/feed-app/app/components/ui/CategoryBadge.tsx`):
- Takes category name as input
- Renders a colored badge using Tailwind classes from categoryList
- Used in CardGrid, TweetCard, and SourcesGrid

**CategoryFilter** (in `home.tsx`):
- Renders category pill buttons in a horizontal scrollable layout
- Only visible in non-deck mode when no source is selected
- Clicking a category updates the URL query param `category=<slug>`

### 4. **Routing and URL Parameters**

**Home page** (`/app/routes/home.tsx`):
- Query parameters:
  - `category` (slug) — filters by categorySlug in sources table
  - `source` (id) — filters by source ID
  - `kind` (articles|releases|video|podcast|twitter) — filters by source.kind
  - `period` (All|Today|Month) — client-side filtering only
- No dedicated category pages — all category filtering is done via query params on the home route

### 5. **Data Flow: D1/Drizzle to Frontend**

**Deck Mode (default landing):**
1. Home loader checks URL params and determines if `isDeckMode` (all filters are "all")
2. If deck mode:
   - Queries all active categories (excluding "all")
   - For each category: runs a parallel query joining `radarItems` + `sources` where `sources.categorySlug = cat.slug`
   - Returns `deckData: DeckData` (record of category slug → {items, hasMore})
3. DeckView renders one column per category with initial items
4. Each DeckColumn can load more items via `/api/radar-items?category=<slug>&page=<n>`

**Filtered Mode (when category/source/kind filter is active):**
1. Home loader builds SQL conditions from query params
2. Queries radarItems + sources with JOINs and WHERE clauses
3. Returns items with category/categorySlug attached from the sources table
4. Client-side pagination via `/api/radar-items` API endpoint
5. Period filtering done client-side after API response

**API Endpoint** (`/app/routes/api/radar-items.ts`):
- Accepts query params: `page`, `category`, `source`, `kind`
- Builds WHERE clause from conditions
- Returns paginated results (20 per page) with category data from JOIN

### 6. **How DeckView Integrates Categories**

DeckView (in `DeckView.tsx`):
- Takes `deckData: DeckData` (a record mapping category slugs to items)
- Filters to only show categories that have items
- Renders one `DeckColumn` per active category
- Each column:
  - Displays category name in header
  - Shows up to 10 initial items
  - Supports lazy-loading more items via Intersection Observer
  - Applies period/kind filters client-side

**DeckData structure:**
```typescript
type DeckData = Record<string, DeckColumnData>;
interface DeckColumnData {
  items: RadarItemWithCategory[];
  hasMore: boolean;
}
```

### Key Insights

1. **Categories are static** — defined in code, not in database
2. **Category assignment** — happens at the source level (each source has a fixed category)
3. **No individual category pages** — categories are accessed only via query params on home route
4. **Dual mode rendering**:
   - **Deck mode**: Kanban-style columns for all active categories (initial load)
   - **Filtered mode**: Grid/timeline for specific category/source/kind
5. **Query params drive filtering**: URL state is preserved and synced via `nuqs` (Next.js URL state library)
6. **Color coding**: Each category has a dedicated Tailwind color for badges throughout the UI
7. **Client-side period filtering**: Period filtering (Today/Month/All) happens after data is fetched, not in the database query

### File Locations Summary

| Purpose | Path |
|---------|------|
| Category definitions & types | `/app/data/types.ts` |
| Database schema | `/db/schema.ts` |
| Home page with deck/filter logic | `/app/routes/home.tsx` |
| API pagination endpoint | `/app/routes/api/radar-items.ts` |
| DeckView component | `/app/components/feed/DeckView.tsx` |
| CategoryBadge UI | `/app/components/ui/CategoryBadge.tsx` |
| Layout with sidebar | `/app/components/layout/AppLayout.tsx`, `/Sidebar.tsx` |
| Card displays | `/app/components/feed/CardGrid.tsx` |

Perfect! Now I have comprehensive information. Let me compile a detailed research summary for you.

## NIP-51 Lists Specification Research Summary

I've completed research on the NIP-51 Lists specification for Nostr. Here's what I found:

### 1. Kind 30000 - Parameterized Replaceable Events Structure

**NIP-51 Overview:**
- Defines lists of items that users can create with public or private references
- Lists are stored as parameterized replaceable events (kinds 30000-39999)
- Only the latest event for each unique (kind, pubkey, d-tag) combination is kept by relays

**Event Structure for Kind 30000:**
```json
{
  "kind": 30000,
  "tags": [
    ["d", "list-identifier"],
    ["p", "pubkey1"],
    ["p", "pubkey2"],
    ["e", "event-id"],
    ["a", "kind:pubkey:d-tag"],
    ["r", "https://example.com"],
    ["title", "My List Title"],
    ["image", "https://example.com/image.png"],
    ["description", "List description"]
  ],
  "content": "{encrypted private items}",
  "created_at": 1234567890,
  "pubkey": "author-pubkey"
}
```

### 2. How `d` Tags Work for Categorization

- **Purpose:** The `d` tag serves as a unique identifier for parameterized replaceable lists
- **Format:** A single string value that identifies the specific list within a kind
- **Replaceability:** Only one event per (kind, pubkey, d) combination is stored; newer versions replace older ones
- **Example:** `["d", "mute-list"]`, `["d", "bookmarks"]`, `["d", "custom-feeds"]`
- **Use Case:** Users can create multiple lists of the same type, each with a different d-tag value

### 3. How `p` Tags Store Pubkeys

- **Public Storage:** p-tags are stored in the event's `tags` array as plain text
- **Format:** `["p", "hex-encoded-pubkey"]` optionally followed by relay URL and marker
- **Full Format:** `["p", "pubkey", "relay-url", "marker"]` where marker can indicate purpose
- **Example:** `["p", "3bf0c63fcb93463407af97a5e5ee64fa883d107ef9e558472c4eb9aaaefa459d"]`
- **Other Identifiers:** Sets can also use:
  - `["e", "event-id"]` - for event references
  - `["a", "kind:pubkey:d-tag"]` - for addressable event references
  - `["r", "url"]` - for URL references

### 4. Creating/Updating/Publishing Events

**Creation Process:**
1. Build an unsigned event with kind 30000 (or appropriate list kind)
2. Include a `d` tag with the list identifier
3. Add `p`, `e`, `a`, or `r` tags for public items
4. For private items: JSON array of tags → stringify → encrypt with NIP-44 → store in content field
5. Sign the event with the user's private key (via NIP-07 extension or other signer)
6. Publish to relays using `rxNostr.send(signedEvent)`

**Updating Process:**
- Publish a new event with the **same (kind, pubkey, d) coordinate**
- Append new items to the end of the list (maintain chronological order)
- Relay automatically replaces the old event with the new one
- New version takes precedence

**Deletion:**
- Publish a new event with the same coordinate but with empty/cleared tags

### 5. Encryption Details (NIP-44)

**Private Items Storage:**
```json
{
  "content": "nip44-encrypted-json-string",
  "tags": [
    ["d", "list-id"],
    ["p", "public-pubkey"]
  ]
}
```

- Private tags are structured as JSON array: `[["p", "pubkey"], ["e", "event-id"]]`
- Encrypted using NIP-44 with shared key derived from: author's pubkey + author's private key
- Stored in the `content` field as encrypted string
- **Backward Compatibility:** Clients detect NIP-04 vs NIP-44 by checking for "iv" in ciphertext

### 6. rx-nostr Specific Considerations

From the **feed-app** codebase analysis:

**Current Implementation (useNostr.ts):**
```typescript
import { createRxNostr, createRxForwardReq, createRxBackwardReq } from "rx-nostr";
import { verifier } from "rx-nostr-crypto";

// Initialize
const rxNostr = createRxNostr({ verifier });
rxNostr.setDefaultRelays(NOSTR_RELAYS);

// Fetch kind:3 (contact list)
const contactReq = createRxBackwardReq();
rxNostr.use(contactReq).subscribe({
  next: (packet) => {
    for (const tag of packet.event.tags) {
      if (tag[0] === "p") pubkeys.push(tag[1]);
    }
  }
});
contactReq.emit([{ kinds: [3], authors: [hex], limit: 1 }]);

// Publish event
rxNostr.send(signedEvent);
```

**For NIP-51 Lists (kind 30000):**
- Use `createRxBackwardReq()` for fetching historical lists
- Use `createRxForwardReq()` for real-time updates
- Filter by: `{ kinds: [30000], authors: [pubkey], "#d": ["list-id"], limit: 1 }`
- Include `uniq()` operator from rxjs to deduplicate events
- Call `.over()` on requests to signal completion

**Versions Used in feed-app:**
- `rx-nostr@^3.6.2`
- `rx-nostr-crypto@^3.1.3`
- `nostr-tools@^2.23.3`

### 7. Standard List Kinds (Non-Parameterized)

These use simple replaceable events (kinds 10000-10102), not parameterized:
- Kind 10000: Mute list
- Kind 10001: Pin list
- Kind 10003: Bookmark list
- Kind 10004: Community membership

### Sources

- [NIP-51 Specification (nips.nostr.com)](https://nips.nostr.com/51)
- [NIP-51 Specification (nostr-nips.com)](https://nostr-nips.com/nip-51)
- [NIP-51 GitHub](https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/blob/master/51.md)
- [NIP-33: Parameterized Replaceable Events](https://nostr.co.uk/nips/nip-33/)
- [rx-nostr GitHub Repository](https://github.com/penpenpng/rx-nostr)
- [rx-nostr npm Package](https://www.npmjs.com/package/rx-nostr)
- [E2Encrypted NIP-51 Documentation](https://www.e2encrypted.com/nostr/nips/51/)
